coredata.dc:title: Ultrahigh-luminosity white-light-emitting devices based on edge functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Dae Hun
Kim, Tae Whan
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received considerable attention as excellent candidates for promising applications in multifunctional optoelectronic devices. The synthesis method for GQDs has been investigated in an attempt to find an optimal method for fabricating white GQDs (WGQDs) suitable for use in display systems. However, the emission wavelengths of WGQDs are still not sufficient to cover the range of wavelengths required by lighting sources, and the luminance of the light-emitting devices (LEDs) based on the WGQDs is still lower than that of white LEDs (WLEDs) fabricated utilizing organic molecular phosphors. Here, we report high-luminosity WLEDs based on edge functionalized GQDs (EF-GQDs) formed by using a simple solution method. The white light emission of the EF-GQDs originates from the various energy states between the octadecylamine functional group and the graphitic domain. The maximum luminance and the Commission Internationale de l’Enclairage coordinates for the WLEDs based on EF-GQDs are 464 cd/m2 and (0.27, 0.29), respectively. The luminosity of 464 cd/m2 is, to the best of our knowledge, the highest value ever reported for WLEDs based on GQDs. This successful demonstration of efficiency enhancement for WLEDs based on EF-GQDs can improve our understanding of the mechanisms at play in these devices and lead to their promising applications as backlights in flat panel displays and lighting sources.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dot
Graphene
White light-emitting device
White graphene quantum dot
External Luminance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication process for the WLED with EF-GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology ( 2016R1A2A1A05005502 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis processes for the EF-GQDs.
(a) Low-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the GQDs and high-resolution TEM images of (b) the GQDs and (c) the EF-GQDs.

(a) C1s peaks for the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectra for the GQDs and the EF-GQDs. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy spectra for (b) the GQDs and (c) the EF-GQDs. (d) Photoluminescence spectra with different excitation wavelengths for the EF-GQDs and (e) their energy levels as functions of the wavelengths.
Absorption, excitation and photoluminescence spectra for EF-GQDs.
Normalized photoluminescence decay as a function of the time for PVK and PVK:EF-GQDs.

(a) Device structure, (b) schematic energy diagram, and (c) current density – voltage and (d) luminance - voltage curves for LEDs with PVK and PVK:EF-GQDs. (e) Electrolumiance spectra and (f) Commission Internationale de l′Enclairage coordinates for the WLEDs with PVK:EF-GQDs at different luminances.

Comparison of the luminances, external quantum efficiencies, and CIEs between the white light-emitting devices based on graphene quantum dots in this work with those in related references published in recent years.

head.ce:title: Ultrahigh-luminosity white-light-emitting devices based on edge functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic and Computer Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronic and Computer Engineering, Hanyang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received considerable attention as excellent candidates for promising applications in multifunctional optoelectronic devices. The synthesis method for GQDs has been investigated in an attempt to find an optimal method for fabricating white GQDs (WGQDs) suitable for use in display systems. However, the emission wavelengths of WGQDs are still not sufficient to cover the range of wavelengths required by lighting sources, and the luminance of the light-emitting devices (LEDs) based on the WGQDs is still lower than that of white LEDs (WLEDs) fabricated utilizing organic molecular phosphors. Here, we report high-luminosity WLEDs based on edge functionalized GQDs (EF-GQDs) formed by using a simple solution method. The white light emission of the EF-GQDs originates from the various energy states between the octadecylamine functional group and the graphitic domain. The maximum luminance and the Commission Internationale de l’Enclairage coordinates for the WLEDs based on EF-GQDs are 464 cd/m2 and (0.27, 0.29), respectively. The luminosity of 464 cd/m2 is, to the best of our knowledge, the highest value ever reported for WLEDs based on GQDs. This successful demonstration of efficiency enhancement for WLEDs based on EF-GQDs can improve our understanding of the mechanisms at play in these devices and lead to their promising applications as backlights in flat panel displays and lighting sources.

ce:section.ce:para: White light-emitting devices (WLEDs) have been extensively investigated due to the feasibility of potentially applying them as backlights in flat panel displays and lighting sources [1–3]. Various emitting materials for developing inorganic and organic materials, including the rapidly developing inorganic quantum dots (QDs), have been introduced to enhance the performance of WLEDs [4–8]. Among the research on the use of various emitting materials for improving the performance of LEDs, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been particularly attractive as potential emitting materials in next-generation displays due to their superior properties of simple band-gap engineering, low toxicity, high chemical stability and high mobility [9–13]. Several approaches to the synthesis of GQDs and to varying their luminescence wavelength have been used to enhance their physical characteristics [14–17]. The luminescence wavelength for the GQDs can be controlled by changing their size, edge structure, functionalized surface, and doping molecules [14–17]. Furthermore, research on ways to synthesize GQDs with white light emission, denoted as white GQDs (WGQDs), has recently been suggested [18,19]. The WGQDs were formed by using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method utilizing graphite as a precursor [18]. The graphene-oxide QDs were synthesized to achieve a white light emission source with an excitation peak of 340 nm because of nanoscopic aggregation [19]. However, the emission wavelength range of the WGQDs is still an inherent problem that hinders their use as lighting sources, and the optical properties of the WGQDs significantly affect the luminance properties of the LEDs. Therefore, the luminance and the efficiency of WLEDs based on GQDs are still lower than those of WLEDs fabricated utilizing organic molecular phosphors. Even though the highest external quantum efficiency (EQE) values for WLEDs based on GQDs with Commission International de I’Enclairage (CIE) coordinates (0.28, 0.33) at 10 cd/m2 has approached 0.1% [20] and that with CIE coordinates (0.27, 0.29) at 200 cd/m2 has reached 0.2% [18], the wavelength range of emissions from WLEDs based on GQDs is beyond the normal operating range.

Here, we demonstrate ultrahigh-luminosity WLEDs based on edge functionalized GQDs (EF-GQDs) formed by using a simple solution method. The white light emission from the EF-GQDs originates from the combination of various energy states between the functional group and the graphitic domain. The luminance intensity of the WLEDs based on EF-GQDs is much larger than those of previously reported WLEDs based on GQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed to investigate the microstructural properties of the EF-GQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy measurements were performed to investigate nucleophilic substitution in the EF-GQDs. The absorption, excitation, and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured to describe the energy transitions in the EF-GQDs. Current density - voltage - luminance measurements were carried out to investigate the electrical and the optical properties of the WLEDs fabricated utilizing EF-GQDs.

In this work, the EF-GQDs were synthesized by using a simple solution method, and Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of that method. Firstly, nanoscale graphite was added into a mixed acid solution of H2SO4 and HNO3 (volume ratio of 3:1). The mixed acid solution was ultrasonicated for 3 h at 80 °C. As the graphite was oxidized, epoxy groups formed on the graphite. Cutting sheets were formed resulting from the conversion from epoxy groups to the more stable carbonyl groups. This solution was filtered with a 20-nm nano-porous anodisc filter and was neutralized with sodium carbonate. Deionized water was added to the neutralized solution. The GQD aqueous solution was achieved by using further filtration with a dialysis bag. After further dialyses, an octadecylamine (ODA) solution was added to the GQD solution, and the mixed solution was spun by using a spinbar for 24 h at 90 °C. Finally, the product was cleaned to remove the excess ODA adsorbed on the surfaces of the EF-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication process for the WLED with EF-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The sheet resistivity and the thickness of the ITO thin films coated on glass substrates were 15 Ω/square and 150 nm, respectively. The ITO-coated glass substrates were cleaned in acetone, methanol, and de-ionized water at 25 °C for 20 min each by using an ultrasonic cleaner. After the cleaned ITO-coated glass substrates had been dried by using N2 gas with a purity of 99.999%, the surfaces of the ITO-coated glass substrates were treated with an ultraviolet-ozone gas for 20 min at room temperature. After the poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) layer had been deposited on the substrate at 5000 rpm, the poly(9-vinylcarbazole) (PVK): EF-GQD solution was deposited on the PEDOT:PSS layer by using a spin-coating method at 5000 rpm for 41 s, after which the sample was annealed at 145 °C for 15 min in a glove box. After the samples had been transferred into an evaporation chamber, the 2,2′, 2″-(1,3,5-benzinetriyl)-tris(1-phenyl-1-H-benzimidazole) (TPBi) organic layers and the LiF/Al electrodes were deposited at a system pressure of 1.7 × 10−4 Pa. The deposition rates of the organic layers and the metal layer were approximately 1.0 and 2.0 Å/s, respectively.

The TEM images were measured by using a JEM 2100 F transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV. The XPS spectra were measured by using a theta probe base system purchased from the Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. installed at Hanyang LINC+Analytical Equipment Center (Seoul). FTIR spectra were observed by using the Nicolet iS50 system. The absorption and the PL measurements were performed by using an ultraviolet (UV)-visible spectrophotometer (Lambda 650 S) and an X-ray fluorescence spectrometer (TCSPC-FL920) equipped with a pulsed laser operating at 365 nm. The current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics were measured on a programmable electrometer with built-in current and voltage measurement units (M6100, McScience). The brightness intensities were measured by using a brightness meter, and the electroluminescence (EL) spectra were measured by using a luminescence spectrometer (CS-1000, Minolta). All measurements of the electrical characteristics of the LEDs were carried out at 25 °C under atmospheric conditions.

Fig. 2(a) shows a low-resolution TEM image of the GQDs. Spherical GQDs with a uniform size distribution are seen to be dispersed. The diameter of the GQDs is approximately 10 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The lattice fringe of the GQDs is approximately 0.22 nm. The EF-GQDs are formated by ODA chains connecting with GQDs, as shown in Fig. 2(c). The size of the EF-GQDs is almost the same as that of the GQDs, indicative of size stability during synthesis. Therefore, the wavelength white light emitted from the EF-GQDs does not depend on variations in the GQDs‘ sizes.

Fig. 3(a) shows the XPS intensity vs. binding energy plota for the C1s spectra from the GQDs and the EF-GQDs. The dominant peaks at 284.5, 286.1, and 287.6 eV in the XPS spectra for the GQDs correspond to the C-C bonds in the graphene plane, the presence of oxygen functional groups of carbon atoms for the C-OH bonds, and the C-O-C bonds, respectivly [21]. The intensities of peaks related to oxygen functional groups for the EF-GQDs were lower in comparison with those for the GQDs. The decreases in the intensities of the peaks for the oxygen functional groups containing C-OH and C-O-C bonds originate from the nucleophilic substitution between the amine functional groups of the ODA and the epoxy functional groups of the GQDs, resulting in the formation of EF-GQDs. Figs. 3(b) and 3(c) show the FTIR spectra for the GQDs and the EF-GQDs. The peaks at 1212, 1379, 1642, and 3452 cm−1 for the GQDs, as shown in Fig. 3(b), correspond to the C-C, the O-H, the C˭C, and the O-H bonds, respectively, and the multiple peaks between 600 and 900 cm−1 are related to the benzene ring of the graphene plane. The peaks at 1468, 1536, and 3299 cm−1 for the EF-GQDs are related to the CH3/CH2, N-H/C˭N, and N-H bonds, as shown in Fig. 3(c) [21]. While the intensities of the peaks at 1468, 1536, 2917, and 3299 cm−1 related to the CH3/CH2, N-H/C˭N, CH2 and N-H bonds in the EF-GQDs increase due to nucleophilic substitution between the amine functional groups of the ODA and the epoxy functional groups of the GQDs, that of the OH band at 3452 cm−1 decreases.

The nomalized PL spectra for different excitation wavelengths of the EF-GQDs and their energy levels as functions of the wavelengths are shown in Figs. 3(d) and 3(e). The PL peaks of the EF-GQDs shift toward longer wavelength with increasing excitation wavelength. While the excitation peaks of the EF-GQDs shift from 360 to 540 nm, their PL peaks continuously shift from 419 to 594 nm, as shown in Fig. 3(d) (See Supporting Fig. S1 for real PL spectra). The PL peaks for the GQDs move toward a specific peak at 535 nm with increasing excitation wavelength, indicative of energy transfer from the graphitic domain state to the defect site state (Supporting Fig. S2). In constrast to the PL peak for the GQDs, the PL peaks for the EF-GQDs shift to various wavelengths rather than specific wavelengths, indicative of the formation of various emission states due to the functionalized groups on the EF-GQDs.

Fig. 3(e) shows the energy levels of the EF-GQDs. The Fermi level of the GQDs is approximately 4.74 eV and remains almost contant, with no size-related variations [20]. Because the bulk Fermi level of the GQDs is located at the midpont of the gap between the lowest excited state (S1) and the ground state (S0) [22], the energy levels of S0 and S1 relative to the vacuum level can be determined. While the energy levels of S0 relative to the vacuum level shift upward from 6.22, 6.13, 6.08, 6.02, 5.97, 5.93, 5.87 and 5.78 eV with increasing excitation wavelengths from 360 to 540 nm, those of S1 shift downward from 3.26, 3.35, 3.40, 3.46, 3.51, 3.55, 3.62, and 3.70 eV. The white emission of the EF-GQDs originates from the existence of multiple energy gaps, which cover blue to red emissions. Supporting Fig. S3 shows a photograph of green GQDs and EF-GQDs.

Fig. 4 shows the absorption, excitation, and PL spectra for the EF-GQDs and can be used to explore multiple electronic transitions. The absorption spectra show dominant peaks below 250 nm (approximately 4.96 eV), which might be due to transitions from S0 to Sn (n > 2). However, the peaks appearing in the absorption spectra are not present in the excitation spectra. The transitions might be related to nonradiative dissipation via the σ-σ*transition [20]. The dominant PL peak for the EF-GQDs was generated by two peaks in the excitation spectra. All lower excitation peaks approach the dominant peak around 4.8 eV (260 nm) with increasing emission wavelength due to a transition from S2 to S0. While the excitation peaks with higher photon energies shift downward to 3.55 (349 nm), 3.44 (360 nm), 3.42 (363 nm), 3.19 (389 nm), 3.16 (393 nm), and 3.1 eV (400 nm) which correspond to transitions from S1 to S0, the emission peaks move to 2.95 (420 nm), 2.77 (448 nm), 2.68 (463 nm), 2.56 (485 nm), 2.45 (506 nm), and 2.38 eV (522 nm). The emission peaks for the EF-GQDs might originate from functionalzied groups in the graphitic domain. The energy levels of the edge functionalized groups (SED) are located below the S1 levels, resulting in energy transfer from S1 to SED. (See Supporting Figs. S4 and S5 for details.)

Fig. 5 shows the normalized PL decays as functions of time for the PVK and the PVK:EF-GQDs. The PL decays as functions of time for the PVK and the PVK:EF-GQDs can be expressed as double-exponential functions (F(t)) [23]:
(1)
F
( t )
=
A + B
1
exp
(
− t /
τ
1
)
+
B
2
exp
(
− t /
τ
2
)
,
where A is the absorbance at 375 nm after irradiation of the sample, B1 and B2 are the corresponding coefficients, t is the time, and τ1 and τ2 are the characteristics exciton lifetimes, respectively.

The PL decay values as functions of time for the PVK and the PVK:EF-GQDs display two lifetime components: a long and a short exciton lifetime species. The longer lifetime components of the PL decay values as functions of time for the PVK and the PVK:EF-GQDs are 14.39 and 11.68 ns, respectively, and the corresponding shorter lifetime components are 3.13 and 2.01 ns. The decreased exciton lifetime components might be attributed to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) from S1 to SED due to more energy states existing between the functionalized group and the graphitic domain. The FRET efficiency is calculated by using the amplitude-averaged lifetime. The amplitude-weighted average exciton lifetime (τavg) is defined as follows [24]:
(2)
τ
avg
=
∑
τ
k
f
k ,
f
k
=
B
k
/ ∑
B
i
,
where τk and fk are the exciton lifetime and the fractional intensity, respectively. The τavg values of the PL decay values as functions of time for the PVK and the PVK:EF-GQDs are 4.03 and 3.56 ns, respectively. The FRET efficiency (ε) is defined between the host and the guest as follows [25]:
(3)
ε
=
(
1 - τ
avg /
τ
0 , avg
)
/ 100 ,
where ε is the FRET efficiency, and τ0,avg is the amplitude-weighted average exciton lifetime of the PVK. The FRET efficiency for the PVK:EF-GQDs is 11.66%, indicative of energy transfer from the PVK to the EF-GQDs. The fitting parameters and the results for PL decays are summarized in Table S1.

Fig. 6(a) shows the structure of the WLEDs with EF-GQDs. The device structure of the WLEDs is as follows: ITO-coated glass substrate/PEDOT:PSS hole transfer layer (40 nm)/PVK: EF-GQD electron emitting layer (3 wt%, 50 nm)/TPBi electron transfer layer (30 nm)/LiF electron injection layer (1 nm)/Al electrode (100 nm). Fig. 6(b) shows a schematic energy diagram for the WLEDs with EF-GQDs to explain the white emission mechanisms of the LEDs. While the elecrons flow from the Al elecrode to the electron emitting layer (EML), the holes at the ITO elecrode move to the EML due to the applied electric field, resulting in the formation of excitons. The energy of the excitons generated in PVK is transferred to the EF-GQDs via non-radiative dipole-dipole coupling of the FRET. The EF-GQDs are excited by the absorption of energy and then release the excess energy into radiation emission. The energy released from the GQDs corresponds to the energy gap between S1 and S0. Because the Sedg levels in the functionalized goups of the EF-GQDs are located just below the S1 level, energy transfer from S1 to Sedg can take place, resulting in a white emission from LEDs with EF-GQDs. The operating voltages of the LEDs with PVK and PVK:EF-GQDs at 10 mA/cm2 are 16.2 and 8.2 V, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6(c). The maximum lumiances for the LEDs with the PVK at 18.5 V and the PVK:EF-GQDs at 14 V are 15.8 cd/m2 and 464 cd/m2, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6(d). The maximum luminance of the LEDs with the PVK:EF-GQDs is 464 cd/m2, which is the highest luminance to date among those reported for LEDs based on WGQDs [18,20], as shown in Table 1.

Fig. 6(e) shows the EL spectra for the LEDs with the PVK:EF-GQDs at different luminances. When the luminance of the LEDs with the PVK:EF-GQDs is increased from 1 to 464 cd/m2, the dominant peaks correspond to a blue shift from 425 to 480 nm, indicative of energy transfer from the graphitic domain to the functional groups of the edge. The EL intensities of the shoulders at 561 (green) and 602 nm (red) are enhanced with increasing luminance, resulting in the emission of white light. The l’Enclairage CIE coordinates of the white light emitted from LEDs with PVK:EF-GQDs at luminances of 1, 10, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, and 464 cd/m2 are (0.19, 0.16), (0.19, 0.15), (0.19, 0.16), (0.20, 0.17), (0.21, 0.19), (0.22, 0.21), (0.24, 0.24), and (0.27, 0.29), respectively, as shown in Fig. 6(f), indicative of EQE values for those white emissions of 0.32%, 0.39%, 0.37%, 0.20%, 0.21%, 0.19%, 0.10%, and 0.07%, respectively, as shown in Supporting Fig. S6. While the highest lumiance of the WLEDs with the PVK:EF-GQDs is 464 cd/m2, their CIE coordinate and EQE are (0.27, 0.29) and 0.07%, respectively. The highest luminance of the WLEDs studied in this work based on GQDs appears to occur in a normal operating region rather than the abnormal operating regions previously reported [18,20]. The luminance enhancement of the WLEDs with the EF-GQDs originates from the efficient energy transfer between the PVK and the EF-GQDs due to the existence of multiple energy states that can take part in energy transfer with functionalized groups.

In summary, WLEDs based on EF-GQDs were formed by using a solution process to enhance their luminance. The white light emission of the EF-GQDs originated from the various energy states between the octadecylamine functional group and the graphitic domain. The FRET efficiency for the PVK:EF-GQDs was 11.66%, indicative of the occurrence of efficient energy transfer from the PVK to the EF-GQDs. The maximum luminance and the CIE coordinates for the WLEDs based on EF-GQDs were 464 cd/m2 and (0.27, 0.29), respectively, the luminance value being the highest among those reported to date for WLEDs based on GQDs. These results indicate that ultrahigh-luminosity WLEDs based on EF-GQDs hold promise for potential applications in transparent, flexible lighting sources and displays.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (2016R1A2A1A05005502).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2018.06.064.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dae Hun Kim received his Ph.D. degree in electronics and computer engineering from Hanyang University, Korea in 2018. He is currently working as a postdoc at Hanyang University from March 2018. His research speciality is nanoelectronic device and fabrication technology, especially on the interface properties associated with application in next-generation photovoltaic and light-emitting devices.

Prof. Tae Whan Kim received his Ph.D. degree in Department of Physics from State University of New York at Buffalo in 1989. He is the HYU Distinguished Professor in the Department of Electronic Engineering at Hanyang University. His current research interests include memristive devices, organic light-emitting devices, and nanogenerators.
